ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific concept for Nasi Lemak. The nearest parent is a rice dish cooked in coconut milk. A new FoodOn concept representing this Malaysian-Singaporean staple is recommended for future ontology development.
ontology.foodex2_description: Composite rice dishes — rice cooked in coconut milk with traditional accompaniments (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Lemak; A03SL used as the closest defensible parent for composite coconut-milk rice dishes.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Nasi Lemak as of the processing date.

preparation_method: rice cooked in coconut milk with pandan leaf
sambal prepared by frying chilli paste

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Nasi Lemak is approximately 380 g, equivalent to one hawker-centre packet or plate, comprising coconut rice, sambal, one egg, ikan bilis, and peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi Lemak — IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Singapore cross-referenced, Food Code G00011661
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD); cross-referenced with Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD for Nasi Lemak (Food Code G00011661) and cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo database. Both are approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based Dishes — White rice cooked in coconut milk analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Nasi Lemak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white jasmine rice cooked in coconut milk, which has a slightly elevated GI compared to plain boiled rice (~54–58) due to the fat content of coconut milk moderately slowing glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (60 × 72.96) / 100 ≈ 43.8, rounded to 43.7 per input record. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 278 mg per 100 g, equating to approximately 1,056 mg per standard 380 g serving (approximately 46% of the 2,300 mg daily recommended limit), contributed primarily by sambal and ikan bilis.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Nasi Lemak to occasional consumption, request reduced sambal and ikan bilis, and monitor total daily sodium intake carefully given that a single serving provides nearly half the recommended daily sodium allowance.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high glycemic index (GI 60) combined with a high glycemic load per serving (~43.7), driven by the coconut-milk rice base which provides approximately 73 g of carbohydrate per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Nasi Lemak sparingly, limit rice to half a portion, pair with higher-protein and higher-fibre accompaniments such as egg and cucumber, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the high glycemic load per serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content — 5.8 g per 100 g (approximately 22.0 g per standard 380 g serving), primarily from coconut milk, which may raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Nasi Lemak to occasional consumption, choose smaller rice portions, and be aware that the saturated fat from coconut milk in a standard serving substantially exceeds the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g for a 2,000 kcal diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat content from coconut milk may promote pro-inflammatory pathways at high consumption frequency.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Nasi Lemak in moderation; ikan bilis and peanuts provide calcium and healthy fats respectively, but frequent high-saturated-fat intake should be avoided to limit systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi Lemak is the quintessential breakfast and comfort food of Malaysia and Singapore, consisting of fragrant jasmine rice cooked in coconut milk with pandan leaf, traditionally served wrapped in banana leaf with crispy fried ikan bilis (dried anchovies), roasted peanuts, sliced cucumber, a hard-boiled or fried egg, and spicy sambal chilli paste. The coconut milk imparts a rich, creamy fragrance to the rice and a slightly sweet flavour that balances the heat of the sambal. The basic version provides approximately 176 kcal per 100 g, with 4.9 g protein, 9.4 g fat (5.8 g saturated), and 19.2 g carbohydrate. A standard 380 g serving delivers approximately 669 kcal, 1,056 mg sodium, and a glycemic load of approximately 44, making it a high-energy, high-sodium, and high-saturated-fat meal. While originating as a humble Malay breakfast, it is now consumed at all meal times and found at hawker stalls, mamak restaurants, and food courts across Singapore and Malaysia. Clinically, it is unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high glycemic load, sodium content, and saturated fat from coconut milk. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Food Code G00011661) cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database, both approved primary regional sources under Project Veritas v2. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis and confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Nasi Lemak (basic) not separately reported in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the source record. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as confirmed by the verified source record. FoodEx2 code A03SL used as closest parent for composite coconut-milk rice dishes; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Nasi Lemak. FoodOn URI is null as no specific FoodOn concept exists for Nasi Lemak; gap note recommends creation of a new concept. SNOMED CT code is null as no applicable clinical food concept exists. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on white jasmine rice cooked in coconut milk; no direct GI entry for Nasi Lemak exists in the database. Glycemic load per serving of 43.7 calculated as (60 × 72.96) / 100 ≈ 43.8, consistent with input record value of 43.7. Hindi local name नासी लेमाक is a transliteration of the Malay name as no established Hindi culinary term exists for this dish.
